title: Greek Giant Baked Beans

ingredients.name: canned or cooked kidney beans (rajma), drained

subs.name: White kidney beans (safed rajma) or butter beans give a creamier result closest to the original Greek gigantes; canned chickpeas also work with a slightly firmer texture

ingredients.name: ripe tomatoes, finely chopped (or one 400 g can)

subs.name: One 400 g can of crushed tomatoes gives a smoother, more consistent sauce than fresh tomatoes and works particularly well in winter when tomatoes are out of season
The olive oil is key to this dish — it creates the silky sauce texture. Reduce it but do not eliminate it; a mild oil plus a teaspoon of butter is a workable backup
Fresh oregano (2 tablespoons) gives a brighter flavour; a mix of dried marjoram and thyme approximates the same herbal note

steps.text: Heat the olive oil in a heavy-bottomed pot or kadai over medium heat. Add the onion and cook for 6–7 minutes until soft and beginning to turn golden at the edges. Add the garlic and cook for another minute until fragrant.

Add the chopped tomatoes, tomato paste, oregano, thyme, and bay leaves. Stir everything together and cook for 8–10 minutes, stirring occasionally, until the tomatoes have broken down into a rough sauce and the oil is visible at the edges.

Add the drained beans and the water or stock. Season generously with salt and stir to combine. Bring to a gentle boil, then reduce the heat to low, partially cover the pot, and simmer for 25–30 minutes. The sauce should thicken around the beans — if it reduces too fast, add a splash more water.

Taste and adjust seasoning. Remove the bay leaves. The beans should be very tender, the sauce thick and flavourful, and the olive oil should have a beautiful, slightly glossy quality from absorbing the tomato. Scatter fresh parsley on top and drizzle a little extra olive oil before serving.

Serve warm or at room temperature with crusty bread or roti. Gigantes are actually even better the next day after the flavours have deepened overnight in the refrigerator — reheat gently with a splash of water to loosen the sauce.

substitution_notes: rajma → White kidney beans (safed rajma) or butter beans give a creamier result closest to the original Greek gigantes; canned chickpeas also work with a slightly firmer texture; tomato → One 400 g can of crushed tomatoes gives a smoother, more consistent sauce than fresh tomatoes and works particularly well in winter when tomatoes are out of season; oregano → Fresh oregano (2 tablespoons) gives a brighter flavour; a mix of dried marjoram and thyme approximates the same herbal note; olive-oil → The olive oil is key to this dish — it creates the silky sauce texture. Reduce it but do not eliminate it; a mild oil plus a teaspoon of butter is a workable backup
indian_kitchen_adaptation: Canned rajma (kidney beans) from any Indian grocery store is the most practical ingredient here — look for butter beans (white rajma) if you want the closest match to the original. If using dried rajma, soak overnight and pressure cook for 4–5 whistles first. The braising happens in any kadai or heavy pot. Greek herbs (oregano, thyme) are available at large supermarket spice sections; substitute a pinch of mixed dried herbs if needed. This dish pairs well with bread, rice, or even chapati — its flavour is adaptable.
